title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered to run local history walks. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Daniel, The Route Researcher
Daniel volunteered for the history walks after retiring from teaching geography. He spent rainy mornings comparing old maps with present-day streets and bus routes, trying to turn archive material into routes people could actually follow. [47]His first problem was discovering that the lane shown on the original mill walk had been absorbed into a private car park.[/47] Rather than abandon the walk, he found a parallel path that still allowed visitors to see the river bend from a public bridge. [48]His teaching background helped him judge how much information people could absorb while standing outside in the wind.[/48] He learned to keep explanations shorter than his notes, which was painful at first. Daniel enjoyed the moment when walkers stopped looking at their phones and began noticing rooflines, drains and old boundary walls. He says a good route should make ordinary streets feel newly legible without turning the walk into a lecture.
texts.B:
### Aisha, The Family Stories Collector
Aisha became involved when the town archive borrowed photographs from her aunt’s collection for an exhibition. [49]After seeing how carefully the volunteers handled the pictures, she accepted their invitation to record family stories for the walks.[/49] She thought the role would involve dramatic tales, but the most useful memories were often tiny, precise details about shops, smells and bus stops and Saturday errands. [50]She was surprised by how quietly people spoke when describing places that no longer existed.[/50] To protect interviewees, she patiently checked every story before using it in public or online. Aisha’s favourite walk included a bakery that had become a bank, surprisingly, because three former customers described queuing there before school. She did not imitate their voices; she simply let the quotations do the work. The experience taught her that local history is not only dates on plaques, but also the routines people still miss years later.
texts.C:
### Martin, The Accessible Walk Planner
Martin initially joined the walking group after noticing that publicity photos showed only fit adults on steep steps. He used a mobility scooter himself, so he wanted routes that did not quietly exclude people. [51]After one trial walk left two older participants exhausted halfway up a hill, he redesigned the tour around flatter streets and more frequent pauses near benches and cafés.[/51] Some volunteers worried the new route would feel less historic, but Martin calmly proved it still passed several important buildings. [55]He also wrote clear audio descriptions for features that blind visitors could not touch, such as carvings high above doorways.[/55] His proudest moment came at a stop beside a former cinema. [52]A woman who had avoided tours for years said she could finally hear the whole story without worrying about keeping up.[/52] Martin says access is not a special version of history; it is how history reaches everyone.
texts.D:
### Rosa, The Evening Walk Steward
Rosa regularly helped with evening ghost walks, although she cared more about social history than ghosts. [53]She offered because her great-grandmother had worked in the old laundry that formed the final stop on the route, beside a narrow alley.[/53] Her task was practical: keeping groups together after dark, checking numbers at every corner and making sure nobody stepped into the road while listening to a story. [54]The hardest section involved guiding thirty people across two busy junctions without interrupting the guide’s account of a nineteenth-century fire.[/54] Rosa carried a small torch, a first-aid pouch and spare batteries for the guide’s speaker. She also learned which jokes became annoying when repeated every week. [56]At the end of the season, she wrote a steward’s guide explaining where to stand, when to count walkers and which corners were risky.[/56] She says good stewards are noticed only when something goes wrong, which suits her.

questions.question: found that a historic route could not be followed because access had changed?
questions.explanation: Daniel discovered that the old lane had become part of a private car park.

questions.question: used classroom experience to decide how much explanation listeners could manage?
questions.explanation: Daniel’s teaching background helped him judge how much information people could absorb outdoors.

questions.question: accepted a role after observing how carefully personal material was treated?
questions.explanation: Aisha accepted after seeing volunteers handle her aunt’s photographs carefully.

questions.question: was struck by the quiet way people recalled vanished places?
questions.explanation: Aisha was surprised by how quietly people spoke about places that no longer existed.

questions.question: changed a route after a trial showed it was too tiring for some walkers?
questions.explanation: Martin redesigned the tour after older participants became exhausted on a hill.

questions.question: quotes someone relieved to follow a whole tour without anxiety about pace?
questions.explanation: A woman said she could hear the whole story without worrying about keeping up.

questions.question: volunteered because of an ancestor’s connection with one stop on the route?
questions.explanation: Rosa’s great-grandmother had worked at the old laundry on the route.

questions.question: had to move a large group safely across roads while the talk continued?
questions.explanation: Rosa guided thirty people across busy junctions during the guide’s story.

questions.question: prepared spoken descriptions for details that some visitors could not physically examine?
questions.explanation: Martin wrote audio descriptions for high carvings blind visitors could not touch.

questions.question: wrote practical instructions for future helpers doing the same support role?
questions.explanation: Rosa wrote a steward’s guide explaining positions, counts and risky corners.
